segments.s153-g153: شکاریم is the verb phrase with the first-person plural ending -یم: here it functions predicatively as “we are (prey)” rather than an active ‘we hunt’. یکسر is an adverb meaning “wholly/entirely” placed after the verb for emphasis. همه پیش‌مرگ is the nominal group (همه = “all/everyone”) modified by پیش‌مرگ (“forerunners / those who go before [i.e. soon die]”); Persian allows this verb–adverb–subject order in poetry, and there is no overt copula between the noun phrase and شکاریم (the copular sense is carried by the verb form).
segments.s153-g154: This is a paired nominal phrase using parallel structure: سری زیر تاج و سری زیر ترگ. سری here means “head” (i.e. a person’s status) and is followed by the prepositional phrase زیر + noun, literally “under the crown” / “under a rag.” The conjunction و simply links the two contrasted images; the noun phrases are elliptical descriptions of people (i.e. ‘some have their heads beneath the crown, some beneath a rag’). Note ترگ is an archaic/poetic word for a rag.
segments.s153-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); here it precedes the past verb بشنید ('he heard'), so the clause reads 'when he heard'. The subject رستم follows the verb (a common older/poetic word order). سرش is 'his head' (سر + possessive suffix ش) and خیره گشت uses گشت as a copula meaning 'became' (stative), so 'his head became خیره' = 'he was/wascome stunned/astounded.'
segments.s153-g42: This clause has subject first (جهان, 'the world'), then the locative phrase پیش چشم اندرش ('before his eyes') — اندر + ش ('in/within' + possessive) is the archaic locative construction — and ends with تیره گشت, where گشت again means 'became'. The whole means 'the world became dark before his eyes.'
segments.s153-g43: بپرسید is the simple past 'he asked'. زان پس ('after that') is a temporal adverbial (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after'). که آمد به هوش is a subordinate temporal clause ('when he came to his senses'), where آمد is past 'came' and هوش means 'awareness/consciousness.'
segments.s153-g44: بدو is the fused prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او). گفت here is the past verb 'he said' (the provided sense list lacks the verb sense, so no matching sense is available). با ناله و با خروش is an adverbial of manner ('with a moan and with a cry/uproar').
segments.s153-g45: که here introduces the reported question/quotation after گفت (i.e. 'that/which'), followed by اکنون 'now' and the interrogative چه 'what'. داری is 2nd-person present 'you have' (used in reported speech), and ز رستم نشان = 'of/from Rostam, a token/sign.'
segments.s153-g46: This is an optative/wish clause. که again introduces the clause; کم 'small/less' qualifies نامش 'his name'; باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical/poetic), so کم باد نامش = 'may his name be small/diminished'. ز گردنکشان 'among the گردنکشان' (the rebels/proud ones) gives the domain. The whole is a wish/curse: 'may his fame be reduced among the rebels.'
segments.s153-g47: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت = 'he said' — verb sense missing from provided list). ار is the conditional 'if'. ایدونکه (an older/poetic particle) here functions like 'indeed/that' reinforcing the condition ('if indeed that...'). رستم تویی is the protasis: 'you are Rostam' (تویی = 'thou art').
segments.s153-g48: بکشتی is the past second-person form 'you killed' (here used of the addressee). The object pronoun appears split as م + را (m + را) where را is the definite object marker; because the provided senses for م do not match the pronoun, its sense is given as unavailable. خیره here is resultative/participial meaning 'dazed/overwhelmed,' and از introduces the cause 'from/because of' بدخویی 'ill-temper/unkindness' — 'you have killed me, left me dazed by your ill-temper.'
segments.s153-g97: اگر is the conditional 'if' introducing the clause. کشته شد is a perfect/passive construction: the past participle کشته + the auxiliary شد = 'was killed'. رستم جنگجوی is a name with an epithet: the noun رستم followed by the adjective/appositive جنگجوی 'the warrior', a typical poetic apposition (“Rostam the warrior”).
segments.s153-g98: از ایران is a locative/agentive phrase 'from/of Iran' that here qualifies Rustom as 'of Iran' or indicates origin; که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/which'. یارد شدن is a verbal phrase 'to become a helper/ally' (یارد + شدن), with شدن as a verb of becoming. پیش اوی places the action spatially/relationally 'before/in the presence of him' (اوی = 'him').
segments.s153-g99: به انبوه زخمی is an adverbial/accusative phrase: به + انبوه ('many/numerous') modifying زخمی ('the wounded') — 'many wounded'. بباید is the modal auxiliary 'it is necessary / one must', followed by the infinitive زدن 'to strike/kill', so the whole phrase means 'one must strike (down) many wounded.'
segments.s153-g100: برین رزمگه is an archaic demonstrative + noun: بر + ین + رزمگه yields 'on this battlefield' (برین = 'this' attached to the locative). بر نشاید بدن is inversion typical of epic diction: بر = 'on', نشاید = 'it is not fitting / must not', بدن = 'body / corpse'. Together: 'it is not fitting that the body be on this battlefield' (i.e. the body should not be left there).
segments.s153-g101: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آشوب 'turmoil/commotion' is the subject, and برخاست is the past tense 'rose/arose'. از انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the assembly', giving source of the commotion.
segments.s153-g102: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus spoke/so said' (adverb + verb), followed by the subject سهراب. با introduces the person accompanied: پیلتن (with Piltan). Word order (adverb-verb-subject-prepositional phrase) is typical of epic style.
segments.s153-g103: The initial که introduces reported speech ('that'). اکنون که = 'now that' (introducing a subordinate temporal clause). روز من اندر گذشت is idiomatic: روز = 'day' or 'time/fortune', من = 'my', اندر گذشت = 'has passed/has gone by' — 'now that my day (my time/fortune) has passed.'
segments.s153-g104: همه کار ترکان is a noun phrase 'all the affairs of the Turks' (همه 'all' + کار 'affairs' + ترکان 'the Turks'). دگرگونه گشت is a verbal phrase where دگرگونه = 'different/changed' and گشت = 'became/turned', so 'all the Turks' affairs have become different.'
segments.s153-g57: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb) placed at the front to set the time. کارگر شد is a past-tense predicate: کارگر here is adjectival/nominative meaning 'useful/effective' (not necessarily a noun 'worker') and شد is the past/preterite 'became'. که introduces the following clause (here functioning as a connective 'and/so that/whereas'); بیکار گشت is a parallel past clause where بیکار 'idle/unemployed' is the predicate and گشت (an alternative past of شدن) means 'became/turned (into)'. The two clauses are linked to contrast the change of state.
segments.s153-g58: This clause states the effect on the son: word order is [subject (پسر)] [prepositional phrase acting as adverbial: پیش چشم پدر = 'before/in the presence of his father'] then predicate خوار گشت 'became despised'. خوار is an adjectival predicate 'despised/worthless' and گشت is the past-tense verb 'became'.
segments.s153-g59: همان نیز مادر = 'that same (one) also, the mother' — همان 'that/the same' and نیز 'also' emphasize identity and addition; مادر is the subject. به روشن‌روان is a prepositional phrase 'to Roshan‑rovan' (a proper name/epithet), with به marking the recipient/direction.
segments.s153-g60: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' (third-person). با من 'with me' indicates accompaniment; one reads با + object as 'together with me'. یکی پهلوان is a noun phrase 'a certain champion / one champion' where یکی is an indefinite/determinative 'one / a certain' modifying پهلوان. The verb precedes its objects/phrases in this line but the overall meaning is 'she sent (someone), a champion, to accompany me.'
segments.s153-g61: بدان تا is a purposive phrase: بدان (here a fixed adverbial 'to that / for that purpose') + تا 'so that / in order that' introduces the purpose clause. پدر را is the definite object ('the father') marked by را for emphasis/definiteness. نماید is a subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form of نمودن meaning 'may show' or 'might show' (classical Persian uses this form to express purpose or reported intent). به من 'to me' marks the beneficiary/recipient.
segments.s153-g62: سخن برگشاید is a jussive/optative clause: سخن 'word/speech' is the theme, and برگشاید (classical subjunctive of برگشتن/گشودن here meaning 'be revealed / be spoken / be opened') expresses the hoped-for result — 'that the speech may be uttered.' به هر انجمن is a locative/occasional phrase 'in every assembly' (به = in/at, هر = every, انجمن = assembly/meeting).
segments.s153-g63: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آن نامور پهلوان is the noun phrase 'that renowned/famed champion' (آن determiner + نامور 'famed' + پهلوان 'champion'). کشته شد is a passive/perfect construction 'was killed/slain' where کشته is the past participle 'killed' and شد functions as the auxiliary 'became/was' to form the passive.
segments.s153-g64: This is a nominal/experiential result clause: مرا (me / 'for me') placed frontally as the experiencer/topic, نیز هم 'also / likewise' emphasizing addition, روز 'day' as the grammatical subject meaning 'one's day / fortune', and برگشته شد 'was returned / came back' (برگشته past participle + شد auxiliary). Together: 'my day, too, returned' — an idiom for fortune changing in one's favor.
segments.s153-g65: کنون is an adverb placed at the start meaning 'now.' بند is the object 'the band/strap' and بگشای is an imperative (2nd person singular) meaning 'open/undo'; Persian can place the imperative after its object for emphasis. از جوشنم literally 'from my cuirass' uses the preposition از + noun+possessive suffix (-م) to mark what is being unfastened.
segments.s153-g66: برهنه is an adjective 'bare/naked' placed before the verb-like command نگه کن (imperative 'keep/leave'), i.e. 'keep it bare.' تن روشنم is a noun phrase 'my bright/illuminated body' with روشن + possessive -م; adjective follows the noun here but is linked to it by being adjacent, so the whole phrase specifies what is to be kept exposed.
segments.s153-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' بگشاد is an older/poetic past form meaning 'opened' (here used as the event verb). خفتان functions here as the thing being opened/relieved (an archaic/poetic noun-form related to 'sleep' or covering) and is the direct object of بگشاد; و links to the following clause and آن مهره دید is a simple clause '(he) saw that bead/amulet.'
segments.s153-g68: همه جامه is 'all (the) garments' or 'everyone's garments' with همه as a distributive/collective modifier. بر خویشتن uses the preposition بر + emphatic/reflexive خویشتن 'upon themselves,' and بردرید is a past-tense verb 'they tore apart/tore off' (compound past form with a poetic classical ending). The word order places the adverbial/target (بر خویشتن) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s153-g69: همی here is an iterative/progressive particle meaning 'kept (on) / was continually,' and it modifies گفت which in the verse is the past 'said' (note: the provided lemma senses list for گفت is nominal, so the actual verbal past form is being used here). کای is a vocative 'O!' addressing the slain person (کشته), and بر دست من is a prepositional phrase 'in/on my hand' specifying the speaker's relation to the slain.
segments.s153-g70: This is a noun phrase of praise: دلیر و ستوده 'brave and praised' are coordinating adjectives, followed by به هر انجمن which is a prepositional phrase 'in/at every assembly/gathering' qualifying where he was praised. The order adjective + 'به' phrase is typical for attributive complements in Persian poetry.
segments.s153-g71: همی again marks a continuous or habitual action 'was / kept on.' ریخت خون combines the verb ریخت(ن) (here past/progressive 'was pouring/shed') with خون 'blood' to mean 'was bleeding' or 'shed blood.' و links the two actions; همی کند موی uses کند (present/past-form of کندن 'to pull') with موی 'hair' to mean 'tore/pulled at his hair.' The verbs are presented in a compressed, poetic sequence typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s153-g72: سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic possessive -ش). پر ز خاک is a poetic construction 'full of dust' using پر 'full (of)' + ز (poetic preposition 'from/of') + خاک 'dust.' و links the two predicates and پر از آب روی repeats the 'full of' construction but with از (the more common preposition) + آب 'water' + روی 'face,' so literally 'his head full of dust and his face full of water' (i.e., dusty head and tear-wet face).
segments.s153-g105: همه مهربانی بران کن is an imperative: همه مهربانی ‘all/complete kindness’ (direct object) + بران (archaic contraction of بر آن/بر ایشان) meaning ‘toward them’ or ‘to those people’, and کن is the 2nd-person imperative ‘do/show’. که begins a subordinate purposive clause (‘so that’).
segments.s153-g106: This clause gives the purpose/result: سوی جنگ ترکان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward the Turks’ battle/war’ (سوی + جنگ + ترکان). نراند سپاه is a classical verb phrase in which نراند (present/jussive sense here) means ‘does not send/drive’ and سپاه is the direct object ‘army/host.’ Word order puts the goal (سوی جنگ ترکان) before the verb.
segments.s153-g107: که introduces the explanatory clause ‘that…’. ایشان is the subject ‘they’. ز بهر مرا is a classical prepositional phrase ‘for/against me’ (ز بهر = for/on account of; م + را is the old split of مرا = ‘me’ with را marking the object). جنگجوی is the noun complement ‘warriors’ (what they set/sent).
segments.s153-g108: سوی مرز ایران is the direction phrase ‘toward the frontier/border of Iran.’ نهادند is past tense (3pl) of نهادن ‘to place/set/send’ and روی here is ‘face’ (they ‘set/turned [their] faces’ toward that border) — a poetic way to say they directed their forces.
segments.s153-g109: بسی روز را داده بودم نوید is a past perfect construction: بسی روز (many days) + را (topic/definite marker) + داده بودم (I had given — داده = past participle, بودم functioning as auxiliary) + نوید (tidings/good news). The compound داده بودم indicates an action completed prior to the narrative moment.
segments.s153-g110: بسی کرده بودم ز هر در امید is parallel: بسی کرده بودم (I had often done/made) + ز هر در (literally ‘from every door’, an idiom meaning ‘from every quarter/at every approach’) + امید (hope). Again کرده بودم is past perfect (کرده = participle, بودم auxiliary).
segments.s153-g111: نباید که بینند رنجی به راه is an impersonal prohibition: نباید ‘must not/should not’ + که introduces the clause + بینند (subjunctive/present form ‘they see/should see’) + رنجی ‘any suffering’ + به راه ‘on the road/along the way.’ The construction expresses: ‘it should not be that they see suffering on the road.’
segments.s153-g112: مکن جز به نیکی بر ایشان نگاه is an instruction in the negative imperative: مکن ‘do not do’ + جز ‘except’ + به نیکی ‘with kindness’ + بر ایشان نگاه literally ‘(your) look/regard toward them’ — here نگاه is a noun meaning ‘regard/consideration.’ The clause means ‘do nothing to them except regard/treat them kindly.’
segments.s153-g129: چنین (thus) + گفت (said) is a simple past reporting clause: 'He said thus'. با سرفرازان uses با + plural agent to mean 'to/with the honored ones' (addressing a group). که introduces the following subordinate quoted/thought clause ('that I ...').
segments.s153-g130: نه ... نه is a correlative negative: نه دل دارم = 'I have no heart' (دارم = present/personal possession, here used in a present-tense sense though in a reported past context). امروز 'today' qualifies the following clause. گویی here is the particle 'it seems / as if' (poetic), linking to نه تن 'not even a body' — i.e. 'it seems I haven't even a body.'
segments.s153-g131: شما is the addressed plural 'you'. جنگ ترکان is a genitive noun phrase 'war of the Turks' (object). مجویید is a negative imperative/present subjunctive (2nd person plural) 'do not seek'. کس at the end functions as an indefinite ('anyone/anybody') tied to the negation — idiomatically marking lack of need for others to pursue it.
segments.s153-g132: همین بد 'this very bad/ill thing' is the topic; که من کردم is a relative clause 'that I did' (کردم = past first person). امروز 'today' timestamps the action. بس at the end is an exclamatory/evaluative 'enough' — 'that is sufficient.'
segments.s153-g133: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگشت is past 3rd-sg 'returned'. ازان جایگه = 'from that place' (ازان = 'from that', جایگه = 'place/spot'). پهلوان is the subject 'the champion/hero' mentioned.
segments.s153-g134: بیامد is past 3rd-sg 'came'. بر + noun normally means 'to/toward/upon', so بر پور = 'to the son/descendant'. خسته روان is a descriptive phrase 'weary/exhausted in spirit' (خسته modifying روان 'soul/spirit' = 'spirit-weary').
segments.s153-g135: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' is the plural subject. برفتند is past 3rd-plural 'they departed/they went'. با او بهم = 'with him together' (با او = 'with him', بهم = 'together').
segments.s153-g136: چو (repeated) is the comparative/simile 'like/such as' introducing examples; طوس and گودرز are proper names (heroes). و is the coordinating 'and'. چون گَستهم names another companion (گستهم = Gostaham, a proper name) — here چون + name lists those who accompanied him.
segments.s153-g25: نشان داد (nashān dād) is a past tense verb phrase 'showed/gave a sign'. Here مادر (mādar) is the agent/subject placed after the verb for poetic emphasis, مرا (marā) is the first-person object pronoun 'me' attached after the verb phrase, and از پدر (az pedar) is a prepositional/genitive phrase modifying نشان ('the sign of/from my father'). Literal order: [object - verb - subject - object pronoun - source].
segments.s153-g26: ز مهر (z mehr) gives the source 'from/through love/affection'. اندر آمد (andar āmad) is a compound literary past 'entered/came in'. روانم (ravānam) is the possessed noun 'my soul/spirit' and بسر (be-sar / be-sar) here functions as a verbal/locative complement meaning 'to come to life / to settle in (the head)' — together: 'from love my soul entered/was born into (my) head/being.' Word order is typical of classical Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s153-g27: هرآنگه که (har āngeh ke) = 'whenever/whenever it is that'; تشنه شدستی (tashneh shodasti) is an archaic/poetic past 2nd-person form meaning 'you became thirsty' (شدی + archaic ending), and به خون (be khun) marks the object 'for/to blood'. The clause is a conditional/time clause setting up repeated occasions.
segments.s153-g28: بیالودی (biyāludi) here functions as a past verbal/participial form 'you stained/you made bloody' (poetic usage); آن خنجر آبگون (ān khanjar ābgun) is a noun phrase 'that bluish dagger', with آبگون after خنجر as an adjective. The clause states the repeated result: on those occasions you bloodied that bluish dagger.
segments.s153-g29: زمانه (zāmane) is a personified noun 'the time/age/the world' and is subject; به خون تو (be khun-e to) 'for/with your blood' is the object of desire; تشنه شود (tashneh shavad/shavad — here شد is the literary present/subjunctive of شدن) expresses 'becomes/is made thirsty'—a poetic present/future sense ('will be thirsty for').
segments.s153-g30: براندام تو (bar-andām-e to) = 'on/over your body' (بر + اندام gives a locative 'on the body'); موی (mūy) 'the hair(s)' is the noun subject that takes the predicate دشنه شود (doshneh shavad) 'will become daggers' — again شود is the poetic present/subjunctive used as a future/potential. The image: the hairs of your body turn into daggers.
segments.s153-g31: کنون‌گر (konun-gar) is a compound conditional particle 'now if' or 'if (even) now'; تو در آب ماهی شوی (to dar āb māhī shavi) is a conditional clause with شوی (shavi) the present/subjunctive 'you become' used to mark a hypothetical: 'you become a fish in the water.' Word order places the locative phrase در آب before ماهی ('fish in the water').
segments.s153-g32: وگر چون شب اندر سیاهی شوی: وگر (vagar) = 'and if / but if', چون (chun) 'like/as', شب اندر سیاهی (shab andar siyāhī) 'night in blackness/darkness', and شوی again is the subjunctive 'you become'. The clause offers the alternative hypothetical: 'or if you become like the night in darkness.'
segments.s153-g121: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. زان گونه literally 'in that manner/so' (زان = 'that', گونه = 'manner'). دیدند is the past plural verb 'they saw'. بر خاک سر is a prepositional phrase 'with/on the head on the ground' (بر = 'on', خاک = 'earth/dust', سر = 'head'); word order is poetic (preposition + noun + noun). The whole segment sets the time/scene: 'When they saw (him) thus, with his head on the earth...'.
segments.s153-g122: This is a compact, poetic description: دریده modifies جامه ('a torn garment'), and خسته describes the person ('weary'). The short word برو here is a poetic/clitic form corresponding to بر او ('on him' / 'upon him') linking the adjective to the person; the final بر repeats the prepositional idea ('upon'). Overall: 'with a torn robe upon him and (being) weary (upon)'.
segments.s153-g123: به پرسش گرفتند is an idiom meaning 'they questioned' or 'they took him to question' (به + noun پرسش + گرفتن). کاین کار چیست is an embedded interrogative: کاین = 'this/that', کار = 'deed/act', چیست = 'what is (it)?' The verb گرفتند is past plural 'they took/they put'.
segments.s153-g124: ترادل is a noun meaning 'reciprocity/alternation' or an exchange/reciprocal action; here it names the action they are asking about. برین گونه = بر این گونه 'in/for this manner' (the element ین is the clitic/variant of 'این'). از بهر کیست = 'for whose sake / for whom is it?' (کی = 'who', ست = copula 'is'). The whole clause asks who is the object/intended beneficiary of this mutual action shown in that manner.
segments.s153-g125: بگفت is simple past 'he said' (poetic contracted form). آن شگفتی 'that marvel/astonishing thing' is the direct object; که خود کرده بود is a relative clause 'which he himself had done/made' — کرده بود is past perfect (had done), and خود emphatically marks the agent as 'he himself'.
segments.s153-g126: گرامی‌تر is the comparative 'more honored/more dear' (تر = comparative suffix). خود is again the emphatic 'himself/own'. بیازرده بود is a past perfect construction meaning 'had remained unhurt / had not been harmed/angered' (بی- + ازرده). Together: 'he had kept his own honor more and remained uninjured.'
segments.s153-g127: همه 'everyone' is the subject. برگرفتند (past plural) here means 'they took up' or 'they raised' (with reference to a cry). با او 'with him' indicates accompaniment. خروش is the noun 'cry/uproar' and functions as the thing they raised: 'Everyone raised a cry with him.'
segments.s153-g128: This is two parallel nominative phrases joined by و: زمین پر خروش = 'the earth full of uproar' (زمین = 'earth', پر = 'full (of)', خروش = 'uproar'), و هوا پر ز جوش = 'and the air full of surge' (هوا = 'air/atmosphere', پر ز = 'full of', جوش = 'surge/boiling/passion'). The construction uses پر (full) after the noun, a common poetic placement.
segments.s153-g89: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the source of the sound; لشکر is its noun 'the host/army'. برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' placed after the subject (a common poetic order). سراسر 'throughout' modifies خروش, so سراسر خروش = 'a cry/uproar everywhere'.
segments.s153-g90: زمانه here means 'the scene/the world' (the gathered time/setting) and is the semantic subject; یکایک 'one and all / each and every' emphasizes totality. برآمد again is the past 'arose' used impersonally; به جوش is a prepositional phrase 'into a boil/ferment' (به + noun = 'into (a) surge').
segments.s153-g91: بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/said' with the ruler's name following it (verb-before-subject, poetic inversion). تا introduces a subordinate/purposive clause 'that/so that', so تا بوق و کوس = 'that trumpets and drums (be sounded)'.
segments.s153-g92: دمیدند is a past plural 'they blew' (soldiers/buglers understood), joined by و 'and' to the next clause. آمد is the simple past 'came'; سپهدار طوس is the title + name 'Commander Tūs' (verb before the noun phrase is a common poetic order).
segments.s153-g93: ازان پس 'after that/thereupon' sets the temporal frame. بدو is the clitic-like object 'to him'. گفت is the verb 'said' (note: the supplied sense list did not include the verbal sense here). کاووس شاه follows in inverted order as the speaking subject/title.
segments.s153-g94: کز introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'. ایدر 'here' marks place. هیونی 'the savage/monster' is the noun referred to, and سوی رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the battlefield' (سوی = toward, رزمگاه = battlefield). Word order is compressed and poetic, so the clause reads roughly 'that from here the savage toward the battlefield...'.
segments.s153-g95: بتازید is the imperative (2nd person plural) 'charge/ride on/rush'. تا introduces a purposive clause 'so that/in order that'. کار سهراب چیست is an interrogative nominal clause 'what is Sohrab's affair/deed' — چی (what) + ست (archaic/copulative 'is') form the poetic 'چیست'.
segments.s153-g96: که here gives the reason/explanatory sense 'for/because'. بر شهر ایران 'over the city of Iran' uses بر = 'over/on' with شهر ایران. بباید is the modal 'one must/it is necessary', followed by گریست (bare verb 'to weep/lemn) — the construction بباید + bare verb yields 'must weep' (archaic poetic word order).
segments.s153-g1: دگر باره is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning “once again / anew” (دگر = again, باره = instance). اسپان is the direct object (plural ‘horses’). ببستند is past tense, 3rd person plural (“they bound/tied/fastened”); سخت here is an adverb modifying ببستند meaning “firmly / tightly.” Word order is adverbial phrase → object → verb → manner.
segments.s153-g2: به سر is a locative phrase meaning “upon the head(s).” همی گشت is a classical/perfective-present style construction: همی is a continuative/auxiliary particle and گشت is 3rd singular of گشتن “turn/become,” so together “turned.” بدخواه بخت is a noun phrase “ill‑wishing fate” (بدخواه modifying بخت). بر here strengthens the idea of ‘upon/against.’ The whole clause means ‘ill‑wishing fate turned upon the heads.’
segments.s153-g3: به کشتی گرفتن is an instrumental/infinitival phrase meaning “to engage in wrestling” (کشتی = wrestling, گرفتن here part of the compound meaning ‘to take up / do’). نهادند is the past plural of نهادن “to set/place” used idiomatically as ‘they began/initiated’ (i.e. ‘they set about wrestling’). سر is the noun ‘head’ and functions as the part grasped in the wrestling action.
segments.s153-g4: گرفتند is past 3rd plural of گرفتن “they seized/took hold.” هر دو is “each/both,” and دوال (an older form meaning ‘twofold / both’) reinforces that meaning; کمر is the object ‘waist.’ The phrase describes both wrestlers seizing each other’s waists.
segments.s153-g5: هرآنگه که is a subordinating temporal conjunction meaning “whenever / whenever that.” خشم آورد uses آورد (3rd singular) ‘brought/caused’ in a habitual/general sense (‘anger came/was brought’). بخت شوم is a nominal phrase ‘ill‑omened / ill‑fated fortune’ (بخت = fate, شوم = ill‑omened). Grammatically this is a temporal clause introducing the resulting action.
segments.s153-g6: کند is 3rd singular present/past- habitual of کردن (“he/it makes/does”). سنگ خارا is a compound meaning “hard/flinty stone” (خارا qualifies سنگ). به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the manner/likeness of” (به introducing a comparative, کردار here ‘manner/form’), and موم is ‘wax.’ So the clause means ‘(ill‑fated) fortune makes flinty stone as if it were wax.’
segments.s153-g7: سرافراز سهراب is a nominative noun phrase ‘the proud / lofty Sohrab’ (سرافراز = proud/lofty, سهراب = proper name). با زور دست is an instrumental prepositional phrase ‘with the force/strength of (his) hand’ (با = with, زور = strength, دست = hand). The whole segment describes Sohrab’s proud physical power.
segments.s153-g8: تو گفتی is literally ‘you said’ (تو = you; گفتی here functions as 2nd singular past of گفتن “to say” in the line, though the provided senses list lacks that verbal sense). سپهر بلندش ببست: سپهر بلند = ‘the lofty/heavenly sky’; ش is the enclitic pronoun ‘-him/its’ attached in the line (classical placement may vary in poetry), and ببست is 3rd singular past of بستن ‘he/it bound/closed.’ The clause therefore reports speech: ‘you said the lofty heaven had bound him.’
segments.s153-g49: ز هر گونه‌ای is a prepositional/partitive phrase meaning 'in/with every kind/way'. بودمت is an archaic past form 'بودم' + enclitic 'ـت' (to you) meaning 'I was (to you)'; the predicate (رهنمای, 'guide') follows, so the whole phrase reads 'I was a guide to you in every way.'
segments.s153-g50: نجنبید is a negative imperative/jussive ('do not move' / 'do not stir'), followed by an adverbial phrase یک‌ذره ('even a little') modifying the verb. مهرت is مهر + enclitic ـت ('your affection/love'), and ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from its place' — together: 'do not let your affection move a bit from its place.'
segments.s153-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; برخاست is past 'rose/was raised'. آواز کوس is a genitive-like sequence 'the sound/cry of the drum' (آواز qualified by کوس). از introduces a source ('from/at'), and درم here is a possessive/locational form ('my door' in older usage) — literally 'when the drum's sound rose from my door.'
segments.s153-g52: بیامد is simple past 'came' / 'appeared'. پر از خون is a prepositional adjective phrase 'full of blood' placed before the noun ('came full of blood'). دو رخ مادرم is a possessive phrase 'my mother's two cheeks/faces' (رخ = cheek/face) with the enclitic ـم marking 'my'. The overall order is somewhat poetic: verb + adjective phrase + noun.
segments.s153-g53: همی here is a classical particle adding immediacy/emphasis (roughly 'then' / 'just then'). جانش is جان + enclitic ـش ('her life'). از رفتن من is a causal/temporal gerund phrase 'because of / at my going' (رفتن used as a verbal noun). بخست is a poetic past 'shut/closed' used figuratively for 'her life ceased / she collapsed/died' — so: 'then her life closed because of my leaving.'
segments.s153-g54: یکی مهره is a simple numeral + noun 'one bead' (مهره here = bead/amulet). بر بازوی من is the locative phrase 'upon my arm' (بر = on; بازوی = arm; من = my / me). ببست is past third-person 'tied/bound' (from بستن): 'she tied one bead on my arm.'
segments.s153-g55: مرا گفت is the reporting phrase 'she said to me' (مرا = to me; را is the object marker attached in the listing). کاین is the conjunction introducing quoted content ('that/this'), and از پدر یادگار is the noun phrase 'a keepsake/memento from (your) father' (از = from; پدر = father; یادگار = memento).
segments.s153-g56: بدار is an imperative 'keep it' (object omitted but referring to the memento). و ببین = 'and see' (imperative). تا کی آید به کار is a purposive/temporal subordinate clause: تا کی ('until/for how long' — کی here 'when/how long'), آید 'it will come' (present/subjunctive with future sense), and به کار 'to use/into use' — idiomatically 'see how long it will be useful.'
segments.s153-g73: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb (classical syntax). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'; the order is indirect-object + verb + subject (سهراب), so سهراب is the speaker. کین (vengeance) is the theme/topic and بدتریست is a contracted poetic form of بدتر است, an adjective phrase meaning 'is worse' (بدتر + است fused as -است).
segments.s153-g74: به آب دو دیده is a prepositional phrase literally 'with/in the water of the two eyes' (i.e. the tears of both eyes). نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'one should not / it must not,' followed by گریست, here used in a classical/poetic non-finite sense ('to weep' / 'weep') rather than a finite past form — together meaning 'one must not weep.'
segments.s153-g75: ازین (از + این) opens the verbal-nominal phrase 'از این خویشتن کشتن' meaning 'this kind of killing of the self' or 'killing oneself in this way.' کشتن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to kill' and functions as the action whose value is being questioned. اکنون چه سود is an inverted question 'now what benefit (is there)?' with سود 'benefit' as the head noun.
segments.s153-g76: چنین رفت is an idiomatic past statement 'thus it went' or 'so it passed' (رفت used intransitively 'went/passed'). و links the clauses. این بودنی is a verbal adjective/gerundial phrase ('that-which-is' / 'the destined/existent'), modifying کار. کار بود is a simple past copular clause 'it was a matter/affair,' with بود marking past state.
segments.s153-g77: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' خورشید تابان is a noun + postpositive adjective 'the shining sun.' ز گنبد is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the dome,' and بگشت is a past verb meaning 'turned/returned' (poetic/archaic prefix بـ + گشت). The whole clause gives a time frame: 'when the shining sun turned from the dome.'
segments.s153-g78: تهمتن is the subject (proper name/epithet). نیامد is the negative past 'did not come.' به لشکر marks direction/target 'to the army/host,' and ز دشت gives the source/location 'from the plain.' Word order is subject + negative past verb + directional/source phrases.
segments.s153-g79: ز لشکر بیامد is 'came from the army/host' (ز marks source). هشیوار is an adjective 'alert/aware.' The phrase هشیوار بیست uses an archaic/postpositive past copula (بیست = 'was' / a poetic form of 'بود') placed after the adjective to mean 'he was alert.' The whole clause introduces an arriving, alert person.
segments.s153-g80: که تا introduces a purpose or explanatory clause ('so that / in order that'). اندر آوردگه is a locative phrase 'in the assembly/place of bringing' (classical 'آوردگه' = venue/meeting place). کار چیست is an indirect question 'what the affair is,' with چی 'what' and ست (classical copula) 'is' at the end.
segments.s153-g113: نشست is a past tense verb meaning 'he sat' or here 'he alighted'; it appears at the start (verb-first order) as is common in epic Persian. از بر رخش = 'from off his steed' (از = 'from', بر = 'off/upon', رخش = 'steed'); رستم names the owner (Rostam) after the noun (رخشِ رستم). چو + گرد begins a simile/temporal clause ('like/when [as] dust/around...') that connects to the following description.
segments.s153-g114: This is a descriptive clause with the predicative pattern 'پر از X' = 'full of X'. پر (lit. 'full') takes از + noun to indicate what it is full of: 'پر از خون رخ' = 'his face full of blood'. The conjunction و links the parallel phrase 'لب پر از باد سرد' = 'and (his) lip full of cold wind' (i.e. lips affected by the cold). Word order is poetic/inverted: the predicate پر precedes the noun it qualifies.
segments.s153-g115: بیامد is a simple past 'he came'. به پیش سپه = 'into the presence of the host/army' (به = to/toward, پیش here = 'in the presence of'). با خروش is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with a cry' describing how he arrived.
segments.s153-g116: دل از کرده خویش is a causal/experiential phrase: دل ('his heart') + از ('because of') + کرده ('what he had done' — here a noun-like use) + خویش (possessive 'his', written as خوی + ش). So 'his heart, because of his deeds'. با درد و جوش gives the state of that heart: 'with pain and with an inward surge/anguish' — جوش here denotes emotional agitation.
segments.s153-g117: چو دیدند is a temporal clause 'when they saw' (چو = 'when'), with دیدند (past, 3rd pl.). ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians'. روی اوی = 'his face' — اوی is an older/poetic enclitic pronoun meaning 'his'.
segments.s153-g118: همه (all/everyone) is the subject of برنهادند (past 3rd pl. of نهادن with the prefix بر, here 'placed/put' or 'bent/laid'). بر خاک روی is a prepositional phrase 'their faces onto the earth' (بر = onto, خاک = earth, روی = face), i.e. they prostrated themselves with faces to the ground. The verb precedes the prepositional complement in this poetic ordering.
segments.s153-g119: ستایش گرفتند is a fixed verbal phrase 'they took/raised praise' = 'they praised/offered praise'. بر کردگار is the prepositional object 'to the Creator/God' (بر here meaning 'to/toward' in a devotional sense). The verb is past plural, indicating the Iranians' action.
segments.s153-g120: که introduces the content clause 'that he...' (که = 'that'). او زنده باز آمد = 'he came back alive' (زنده = 'alive' predicative before the verb, باز = 'again', آمد = past 'came/returned'). از کارزار = 'from the battle/field' (از = 'from', کارزار = 'battle').
segments.s153-g9: 'غمی بود رستم' is a predicative construction: literally 'sorrow was Rostam' → 'Rostam was sorrowful.' 'بیازید' is an archaic past-form meaning 'opened/unsheathed' (here: 'bared' or 'spread'), and 'چنگ' ('claw') is the image for his hand — so together 'بیازید چنگ' = 'he bared his claw/hand.' Word order is poetic: predicate before the name, and the verb+noun 'بیازید چنگ' follows to describe his action.
segments.s153-g10: 'گرفت' is simple past 'he seized.' 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that' (here 'that one' = the opponent). 'بر و یال' are two coordinated nouns ('head/forepart and mane') forming the locus of seizure, and 'جنگی پلنگ' is an attributive phrase 'the warlike panther' (a figurative epithet for the adversary). Together the phrase means 'he seized that one by his head and mane, the warlike panther.'
segments.s153-g11: 'خم آورد' is a fixed poetic phrasing meaning 'bent (his) back' or 'stooped.' 'پشت' is the body-part 'back' (the thing that is bent), and 'دلیر جوان' is a two-word epithet 'the brave youth/young man' in apposition; the adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian poetry. The whole segment = 'the brave young man bent his back.'
segments.s153-g12: 'زمانه بیامد' literally 'the time/turn came' = 'when the moment came' or 'the tide of fortune arrived.' 'نبودش' is negation + enclitic pronoun ('it/his was not' → 'he had not'), and 'توان' is 'power/ability' — together 'نبودش توان' = 'he had no strength/ability.' The clause expresses a time/result: when the critical moment arrived, he lacked strength.
segments.s153-g13: 'زدش' = 'he struck him' (ز + object clitic -ش). 'بر زمین' = 'onto the ground.' The repeated 'بر' is a poetic usage reinforcing direction/impact. 'به کردار شیر' = 'in the manner/behavior of a lion' ('کردار' = conduct/behaviour, so the whole phrase = 'he struck him down like a lion'). Word order places the manner-phrase after the directional phrase for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s153-g14: 'بدانست' = 'he knew it' or 'he perceived.' The following 'کاو' is an archaic fused conjunction/pronoun (equivalent to 'که او' = 'that he'), introducing the subordinate clause. 'هم' = 'also/too,' and 'نماند' is past of 'ماندن' used here in the sense 'would not remain/continue' (a past-tense report of a future/continued state). 'به زیر' = 'underneath' or 'beneath (him).' So the clause = 'he realized that he too would not remain beneath (him).'
segments.s153-g15: 'سبک' here is adverbial (from 'light') = 'lightly' or 'with a swift/light motion.' 'تیغ تیز' is the noun + adjective 'sharp sword' (poetic adjective after noun). 'از میان' = 'from between/among,' and 'برکشید' is the past form meaning 'drew up/drew out' — in martial contexts it specifically means 'drew (a weapon).' The clause describes the quick drawing of the sword from between them.
segments.s153-g16: 'بر' = 'upon/against' (directional). 'شیر بیدار' = 'the awakened/alert lion' (modifier after noun). 'دل' = 'heart' (direct object) placed before the verb as is common in Persian poetic word order, and 'بردرید' = 'tore apart/cut away.' Together: 'upon the alert lion he tore out the heart.'
segments.s153-g81: دو اسپ 'two steeds' — دو is the numeral placed before the noun; اسپ is an archaic/epic word for a war‑horse (steed). اندر آن دشت is a prepositional phrase 'in that plain' with اندر + demonstrative آن + noun دشت. برپای بود is a predicative phrase: برپای 'standing/erect' + بود (past copula) yielding 'stood upright' (poetic word order places the locative before the predicate).
segments.s153-g82: پر از گرد رستم 'full of Rostam's dust' — پر takes از to mark the substance or cause ('full of'); گرد here means 'dust' (a literal dust‑cloud tied to Rostam). دگر جای بود 'there was another place' — دگر modifies جای ('another place') and بود is the past copula 'was'. The two noun phrases are juxtaposed to describe the scene.
segments.s153-g83: گو پیلتن را چو بر پشت زین — چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. پیلتن را uses را as the definite/object marker (here marking the person/place whose absence is being noticed). گو before the name functions like 'so‑called' / a particle attached to the name (in epic usage); بر پشت زین is a locative phrase 'on the saddle/back' (بر + پشت + زین). The verbal predicate is deferred to the next segment.
segments.s153-g84: ندیدند گردان بران دشت کین — ندیدند is the finite verb 'they did not see' (note the inversion: verb appears before the subject for poetic emphasis). گردان is the subject 'the troop'. بران is a poetic deictic ('on/upon that') attached to دشت 'plain', and کین is an attributive noun 'vengeance', forming 'that plain of vengeance'.
segments.s153-g85: گمانشان چنان بد که او کشته شد — گمانشان is the noun 'their conjecture/ belief' with the possessive suffix شان. چنان بد is a result phrase 'so bad/unfavorable'. که introduces the subordinate result clause 'that ...', and او کشته شد is a passive construction 'he was killed' where کشته is the past participle and شد functions as the auxiliary ('was').
segments.s153-g86: سرنامداران همه گشته شد — سرنامداران 'the leading men/chiefs' is the plural subject; همه emphasizes 'all/everyone'. گشته شد is a compound of the participle گشته + auxiliary شد, producing a perfect/result state 'were become/turned' (here idiomatically 'were gone/perished'). The order places the noun phrase before the verbal phrase, as typical.
segments.s153-g87: به کاووس کی تاختند آگهی — به کاووس is the goal 'to Kavus'. کی here functions temporally ('when' / then) connecting the action; تاختند is the past plural verb 'they rushed' (galloped/rushed). آگهی 'news/information' is the content they carried — the sequence reads as 'they rushed to Kavus with the news ...'.
segments.s153-g88: که تخت مهی شد ز رستم تهی — که introduces the reported clause 'that ...'. تخت مهی is a compound epithet 'the moon‑like / splendid throne' (تخت + adjective مهی). شد is the copular/past 'became'; ز here is the poetic genitive/ablative 'of/from' indicating separation 'from Rostam'; تهی is the predicate adjective 'empty/devoid'. Together: 'that the splendid throne became empty of Rostam.'
segments.s153-g137: همه لشکر is the subject phrase ('the whole/entire host'); از بهر آن ارجمند is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of that esteemed one' — از بهر is an archaic/poetic combination meaning 'for' or 'because of', with آن ('that') followed by the adjective ارجمند ('esteemed'). Word order is typical classical Persian: subject followed by a prepositional/beneficiary phrase.
segments.s153-g138: زبان برگشادند is literally 'they opened (their) tongue(s)' — زبان (speech/utterance) is placed before the verb برگشادند (past, 3rd-person plural 'they opened'), which agrees with the plural implied subject لشکر. یکسر ز بند is an adverbial phrase 'all at once / entirely from the bond' (یکسر = 'at once/entirely', ز = poetic 'from', بند = 'bond/restraint'), indicating their speech was loosed from constraint.
segments.s153-g139: که introduces a wish/optative clause here ('may it be that...'); درمان این کار is a noun phrase 'a remedy for this matter' (درمان 'remedy' followed by the demonstrative phrase این کار 'this affair'); یزدان کند places the divine agent before the verb: یزدان (God) + کند (3rd-sg. present/potential 'does/makes'), yielding 'may God effect a cure'.
segments.s153-g140: مگر here functions as 'unless' or marking an exception. کاین is the fused classical form of که این ('that/this'), introducing the content 'this speech'; سخن بر تو آسان کند is a clause where بر تو is the beneficiary/target ('for you'), آسان is the predicate adjective 'easy', and کند is the 3rd-sg. verb 'make' — 'unless He makes this speech easy for you.' Word order places the object/topic (کاین سخن) before the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s153-g141: یکی دشنه places the numeral/indefinite 'one/a certain' before the noun ('a dagger'); بگرفت is the past-tense verb 'he seized/took' (3rd-sg.), and رستم is the proper-name subject. به دست is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in (his) hand' or 'with his hand' (به + دست), where به here indicates location/instrument ('in/at the hand').
segments.s153-g142: که introduces a purposive or explanatory clause ('so that / with the intention that'). از تن is an ablative 'from the body', ببرد is the 3rd-sg. subjunctive/optative sense of 'take away / cut off' (here glossed as 'might cut off'), and سر خویش پست is the phrase 'his own lowly head' — خویش is formed by خوی + enclitic ش ('his'), with the adjective پست following the noun as is normal in Persian ('head ... lowly').
segments.s153-g143: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones / nobles'; بدو ('to him') is the dative/goal, اندر is an archaic prepositional/adverbial element here emphasizing the contact/location ('upon/onto'), and آویختند is the past plural verb 'they clung / hung on' agreeing with بزرگان. The whole clause means 'the nobles clung to him.'
segments.s153-g144: ز مژگان is an ablative phrase 'from (their) eyelashes'; همی is a classical adverb marking continuing or immediate action (here 'then/so' or 'even so/indeed'), خون فرو ریختند is a compound verbal image 'blood poured down' (فرو + ریختند 'poured downward', past plural agreeing with بزرگان). The line thus pictures blood streaming down from the eyelashes.
segments.s153-g145: بدو (to him) is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' taking گودرز as its subject (inverted word order: prepositional phrase + verb + subject). کاکنون is an emphatic/poetic form meaning 'now', and چه سود is a nominal question 'what benefit/what good' that introduces the direct speech.
segments.s153-g146: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'which'); از روی گیتی is a genitival/locative phrase literally 'from/on the face (surface) of the world' with روی='face/surface' governed by از. برآری is a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning 'you raise/make arise' (here expressing the action attributed to 'you'); تو is the pronoun 'you' placed after the verb (poetic word order), and دود is the direct object 'smoke'.
segments.s153-g147: This clause has a conditional sense without an overt اگر: تو (you) + بر (toward/against) + خویشتن‌گر (self‑willed/selfish) forms a descriptive accusative/advective phrase, and کنی is the (present/subjunctive) verb 'you do/you act', yielding 'if you act self‑willed toward yourself'. صدگزند is a compound noun 'a hundred harms' or 'great harm' that functions as the clause's result.
segments.s153-g148: چه here is an interrogative/exclamatory 'how' introducing the comment; آسانی is the noun 'ease', and آید is the verb 'comes' (present/future sense). ب + دان appear here as the fused prepositional phrase بدان = 'to/for that', followed by ارجمند 'esteemed, noble', so the clause reads 'how easily will ease come to that esteemed one.' (دان as a separate token reflects orthography; together they function as 'to that').
segments.s153-g149: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. ماند is the verb 'remains/is left' and the following او را (he + object/anticipatory marker) marks the person who would remain; به گیتی زمان is a locative/temporal phrase 'in the world for a time' (به = 'in/to', گیتی = 'world', زمان = 'a time/period'), so the whole clause means 'if he remains for a while in the world.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s153-g150: بماند is a jussive/optative third‑person form 'let him remain' (or 'he may remain'); the second clause is an imperative to the addressee: تو (you) + بی‌رنج 'without grief/untroubled' + با او 'with him' + بمان 'stay' (imperative). The pair gives a permissive 'let him stay; you, remain with him unharmed.'
segments.s153-g151: وگر is 'and if' / 'but if' introducing a conditional. زین is the poetic contraction of ز این ('from this' / 'of this') and here functions as part of the locative 'زین جهان' = 'from this world' (orthography treats it as a single token). این جوان is the noun phrase 'this young man', and رفتنیست is a predicative compound meaning 'is to go / is transient / will depart' — together: 'and if this young man is to depart from this world.'
segments.s153-g152: به گیتی نگه کن is an imperative: به گیتی = 'to/at the world' (directing attention), نگه (a verbal noun 'gaze' / 'look') + کن (imperative 'do') together form 'look at the world'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that / so that' and جاوید کیست is an interrogative/explanatory 'who is eternal' (کی 'who' + ست 'is'), so the clause means 'look at the world to see who is everlasting.'
segments.s153-g33: وگر is an archaic/poetic conditional particle equivalent to modern اگر (if). چون ستاره شوی is a protasis: چون (like/as) + ستاره (noun) + شوی (2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن, 'become'); the verb follows the noun as is normal in Persian. بر سپهر is a locative phrase (preposition بر + noun سپهر 'sky/heavens') attached to شوی to indicate location ('become a star on the firmament').
segments.s153-g34: ببری is the 2nd-person singular present/future form of بردن here meaning 'you carry / you would carry off' (classical present tense). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (short form of از). روی زمین is a genitive/locative phrase 'the face/surface of the earth' (روی = face/surface, زمین = earth). پاک modifies زمین ('the pure/fair earth'). مهر is the object 'mohṛ' (here a bright celestial thing — 'the sun' / 'brightness'); the whole phrase means 'you would carry off the (sun/brightness) from the fair face of the earth.'
segments.s153-g35: بخواهد is 3rd-person singular present/future of خواستن 'to want' and here means 'will desire/will demand.' هم (here 'also/even') adds emphasis. از تو is a prepositional phrase 'from you' indicating the target of the desire. پدر کین من is a noun phrase: پدر 'father' modified by کین 'vengeance/hatred' and followed by the pronoun من ('my' = 'me') to indicate possession; collectively 'my vengeful father' or 'my father, who will seek vengeance.' Word order places the verb before the agent phrase for emphasis, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s153-g36: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when.' بیند is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن 'to see' and together چو بیند = 'when he sees.' که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. خاکست بالین من is a verbless nominal clause (typical in poetry) meaning 'the pillow of mine is ashes' (خاکست 'ashes', بالین 'pillow', من = 'my'). The copula is omitted; the clause gives the condition that will trigger the father's desire.
segments.s153-g37: ازین is از + این (here orthographically split) meaning 'from these' or 'from among these.' نامداران is a plural noun 'the famed/renowned ones.' گردنکشان is an attributive plural participial/adjectival compound meaning 'neck-pullers' i.e. 'proud/rebellious ones' — it modifies نامداران, so the phrase reads 'from these renowned, proud-necked men.'
segments.s153-g38: کسی هم is an indefinite subject 'someone also.' برد here is the past 3rd-person singular of بردن 'he carried/took' (classical past), so کسی هم برد = 'someone also carried/took.' سوی رستم is a directional phrase 'toward Rostam' (سوی = toward, رستم = proper name). نشان is the direct object 'a sign/token' — so the clause means 'someone from among them carried a token to Rostam.'
segments.s153-g39: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. سهراب is the proper name Sohrab (subject of the clause). کشتست is a perfect/passive-like form meaning 'has been killed' (کشت + است contracted), so it reports Sohrab's death. و connects to افگنده خوار: افگنده is a past participle 'thrown/cast' and خوار 'low/despised' modifies it — together 'cast low / put to shame.' The sequence reports the state/result (killed and humbled).
segments.s153-g40: ترا را here is the object referring to 'you' (ت = 2nd-person enclitic + را the object marker). خواست is past of خواستن 'wanted/sought.' کردَن after خواست functions as a light/auxiliary verb ('to do' used with خواست to form the verbal phrase 'made/were seeking'); همی is a classical adverb meaning 'still/indeed' or giving progressive emphasis. خواستار is a predicate/adjective 'desirous' or a noun 'claimant/petitioner.' Altogether the segment says 'they sought you, indeed being claimants/eager seekers.'
segments.s153-g17: بپیچید is a past tense 3rd‑person singular verb (پیچید) with a poetic prefix بـ-, meaning ‘(he) turned aside/away’. زانپس is an adverbial phrase ‘thereafter/then’ (classical order places it after the verb). یکی modifies آه and here means ‘one/a single’; آه کرد is a light periphrastic expression (آه + کرد ‘made/did’) meaning ‘he sighed’. The clause therefore sequences action (turned) then reaction (sighed).
segments.s153-g18: ز + noun phrase (ز نیک و بد اندیشه) functions like classical از اینِ «ازِ نیک و بدِ اندیشه» — ‘with regard to/relating to good and bad thought’. نیک و بد are a coordinated adjective pair ‘good and bad’; اندیشه is the noun ‘thought’. کوتاه کرد is a past tense verb phrase (literally ‘made short’) meaning ‘curtailed/stopped’. Word order is the classical prepositional phrase + object followed by the verb (SOV tendency), yielding ‘he cut short his thoughts of good and evil’.
segments.s153-g19: بدو is the archaic/clitic form of به او ‘to him/unto him’. گفت here is the past tense ‘said’ (reporting speech) — the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so the index for that sense is unavailable. کاین (ک + این) introduces the subordinate content clause ‘that this…’. بر من = ‘upon me’ (بر = upon; من = me), and از من رسید is a poetic phrasing meaning ‘reached me/has come to me’ (رسید = arrived/reached). Overall: “He said to him that this had come upon me.”
segments.s153-g20: زمانه here functions as ‘fortune/the state of affairs’ (a common classical meaning). به دست تو is a dative/locative phrase ‘into/into the hand/control of you’ (به = to/into, دست = hand/power, تو = you). دادم is 1st‑person past ‘I gave’, and کلید is the direct object ‘the key’ (used figuratively). The clause therefore states the speaker’s action: ‘I gave the key of fortune into your hand.’
segments.s153-g21: تو is the vocative/2nd‑person pronoun ‘you’. زین is a classical contraction equivalent to از این (‘from/for this’ or ‘because of this’); here it introduces the reason phrase, so it functions like a prepositional/participle marker rather than the literal noun ‘saddle’. بیگناهی is the noun ‘innocence/naïveté’. که introduces a relative/clausal continuation (‘that/which’) and این کوژپشت is the following noun phrase ‘this hunchback’. In short: ‘You, because of this innocence — that this hunchback…’ (introducing the clause that follows).
segments.s153-g22: مرابرکشید is a compound/old poetic verb meaning ‘(he) lifted me up’: مرکب برکشیدن with the enclitic object مرا embedded (مرابَرکشید). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. به زودی is an adverbial phrase ‘soon/quickly’. بکشت is an archaic/poetic verbal form (ب‑ + past stem کشت) used here to indicate that he will/shall kill (the object مرا is omitted but understood). So the clause reads ‘(this hunchback) lifted me up and will soon kill me.’
segments.s153-g23: به بازی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ‘in play/for sport’ (often ‘as a joke’). بکویند is a classical 3rd‑person plural present/reporting form meaning ‘they say/they call out’ (poetic alternative to می‌گویند or گویند). همسال من is ‘my peer/my equal in age’ — adjective noun followed by the possessive pronoun. The whole clause reports a taunt or saying: ‘They, in jest/derision, call (me) a playmate, my equal.’
segments.s153-g24: به خاک اندر آمد is an idiomatic, somewhat inverted poetic sequence: به خاک = ‘to/into the dust/ground’, اندر آمد = ‘came in/arrived’ (i.e. ‘fell to the ground’). چنین is a demonstrative/adverb ‘thus/so’ modifying the clause. یال من ‘my mane’ is the possessed noun (subject) that, by poetic inversion, appears after the verb phrase for emphasis. Literal sense: ‘Thus my mane came into the dust’ (= my mane was thrown down/touched the ground).
